name: Mistletoe Druid
description: Mistletoe, particularly when found growing on oak trees, held immense significance in Celtic Druidic tradition. Known as 'all-heal', it was believed to possess potent magical and medicinal properties. Its rarity, especially on oak, contributed to its sacred status. The Druids revered Mistletoe as a symbol of life, fertility, and protection against evil. Harvesting it was a highly ritualized event, typically performed on the sixth night of the moon using a golden sickle. The severed mistletoe was caught in a white cloth to prevent it from touching the earth, further emphasizing its sacredness and purity. Mistletoe was used in various rituals, including those for healing, fertility, and divination. It was also believed to offer protection against poisons and curses. The plant's association with the oak, a tree sacred to the Druids, amplified its power. It was considered a bridge between the earthly and spiritual realms, facilitating communication with the gods and ancestors. The white berries of the mistletoe symbolized purity and the life force, while the evergreen nature of the plant represented immortality and resilience. Consuming mistletoe was thought to enhance fertility and promote overall well-being. Its presence in the home was considered a ward against misfortune and a symbol of good luck. The Druids' reverence for mistletoe highlights their deep connection to nature and their belief in the inherent power of plants.
subtitle: Sacred Herb of the Druids

keyMyths.title: The Golden Bough
keyMyths.description: The ritual harvesting of mistletoe with a golden sickle highlights its sacredness and connection to the divine. The white cloth catching the severed plant symbolizes its purity and separation from the mundane.

sources.author: Pliny the Elder
sources.description: Describes the Druidic rituals surrounding mistletoe harvesting.
sources.text: Describes the Druidic rituals surrounding mistletoe harvesting.

sources.author: Miranda Green
sources.description: Provides insight into the role of plants in Druidic beliefs and practices.
